standard_description: Produce, expand, and rearrange complete simple and compound sentences (e.g., The boy watched the movie; The little boy watched the movie; The action movie was watched by the little boy).
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Simple and compound sentences
khan_exercises.description: Identify which sentences are simple and which are compound

items.question_content:
**Choose the _simple_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**The robot rolled towards Adebowale.**

choices.content: The robot rolled towards Adebowale.
Adebowale ran as quickly as he could; a robot was rolling towards him.

items.question_content:
**Choose the _compound_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Humphrey spun the globe, and Peggy put her finger on a random country.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Humphrey spun the globe, and Peggy put her finger on a random country.
Humphrey spun the globe and put his finger on a random country.

items.question_content:
**Choose the _simple_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**Tiny sharks, grey and hungry, chased our boat.**

choices.content: Tiny sharks, grey and hungry, chased our boat.
Tiny grey sharks chased our boat; they looked really hungry.

items.question_content:
**Choose the _compound_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Shepherds herd sheep, and goatherds herd goats.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Shepherds and goatherds herd sheep and goats, respectively.
Shepherds herd sheep, and goatherds herd goats.

items.question_content:
**Choose the _compound_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**The aliens collected wheat, and they also collected corn.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: The aliens collected wheat and corn.
The aliens collected wheat, and they also collected corn.

items.question_content:
**Choose the _simple_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

items.question_content:
**Choose the _compound_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

The plates clattered, and a glass fell to the floor and broke.

hints:

items.question_content:
**Choose the _compound_ sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

choices.content: The guitarist rocked hard.
The guitarist rocked hard, but the bassist rocked harder.

This sentence only contains one independent clause, so it's a **simple sentence**.

choices.content: It's a simple sentence.
It's a compound sentence.

khan_exercises.title: Rearranging simple and compound sentences
khan_exercises.description: Identify and arrange simple and compound sentences.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**The rhinoceros lowered its head and charged.
**

choices.content: The rhinoceros lowered its head and charged.
The rhinoceros lowered its head to charge, and the baboons scattered.
Lowering its head, the rhinoceros charged; a flock of birds got out of the way.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**The venus fly trap snapped shut, but the nearby bug narrowly escaped being caught.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "but".

choices.content: The venus fly trap snapped shut, but the nearby bug narrowly escaped being caught.
The venus fly trap snapped shut, catching a bug.
The venus fly trap snapped shut and caught a bug.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**I don't want to go inside this cave full of bats.**

choices.content: I don’t want to go into this cave—I think it’s full of bats!
This cave is full of bats; I don't want to go inside!
I don't want to go inside this cave full of bats.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Juniper went kayaking in the ocean, and she saw a humpback whale.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Juniper went kayaking in the ocean, and she saw a humpback whale.
While kayaking in the ocean, Juniper saw a humpback whale.
Juniper went kayaking in the ocean and saw a humpback whale.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**I have never been especially good at bowling.**

choices.content: I have never been especially good at bowling, but it’s never too late to learn!
I have never been especially good at bowling.
I have never been especially good at bowling, and I hate it.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Gizmo is scared of thunder; he hides under the covers whenever it storms.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: Gizmo is scared of thunder; he hides under the covers whenever it storms.
Whenever it storms, Gizmo hides under the covers.
Gizmo hides under the covers when it storms due to his fear of thunder.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**Hagrid skillfully piloted his flying motorcycle over the skies of Surrey.**

choices.content: Hagrid skillfully piloted his flying motorcycle over the skies of Surrey.
Hagrid loved his flying motorcycle; he skillfully piloted it over the skies of Surrey.
Hagrid loved his flying motorcycle, and he skillfully piloted it over the skies of Surrey.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Sweetie the bull stamped his feet, and he snorted all over the pen.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "and".

choices.content: Sweetie the bull stamped his feet and snorted in the pen.
Sweetie the bull stamped his feet, and he snorted all over the pen.
Sweetie the bull stamped his feet in the pen, snorting as he did.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**The witches stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.**

choices.content: The witches stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.
Even though they had fancy steel ladles, the witches still stirred their cauldrons with long-handled birch sticks.
The witches stirred their cauldrons, and they used long-handled birch sticks.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _simple_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Sentences can be **simple** or **compound**:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.
* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The simple sentence is

>**They make enormous pork sandwiches in this Iowa town!**

choices.content: This town in Iowa is renowned for its enormous pork sandwiches: the filling’s bigger than the bun!
They make enormous pork sandwiches in this Iowa town!
Although they’re not the biggest in the state, this Iowa town has some of the largest pork sandwiches around!

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards. I've never dared to play with her.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, so I've never dared to play with her.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a comma and the coordinating conjunction "so".

choices.content: Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, so I've never dared to play with her.
Stina is extraordinarily good at playing billiards, I've never dared to play with her.
I've never dared to play with Stina; really, extraordinarily good at playing billiards.

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> We wore straw hats to shield our eyes. The sun was blistering.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**We wore straw hats to shield our eyes from the blistering sun.**

choices.content: We wore straw hats to shield our eyes, and the sun was blistering.
We wore straw hats to shield our eyes from the blistering sun.
The sun was blistering; we wore straw hats to shield our eyes.

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that best combines these sentences into a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes. He wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**Nikolai waited very carefully in the brush; he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes to take a photo of the baby bear.
Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes, he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.
Nikolai waited very carefully in the bushes; he wanted to take a photo of the baby bear.

items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> The ponies trotted on the tundra.  They were unaware of the wolves.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**The ponies trotted on the tundra, unaware of the wolves.**

choices.content: The ponies trotted on the tundra, unaware of the wolves.
The ponies trotted on the tundra; they were unaware of the wolves.
The ponies trotted on the tundra, and they were unaware of the wolves.

items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _compound_ sentence.**

> I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store. I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **compound** sentence:

* A **compound** sentence contains more than one independent clause.

We can easily identify **compound** sentences because the independent clauses they contain must be linked with either a *semicolon* or a *comma + coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS).*

The compound sentence is

>**I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store; I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.**

This sentence contains two independent clauses linked by a semicolon.

choices.content: The delicious pie I bought at an Amish country store has apples and peaches in it.
I bought a delicious pie  with apples and peaches in it at an Amish country store.
I bought a delicious pie at an Amish country store; I think it’s got apples and peaches in it.

items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> Angelica likes candy. Her favorites are those little root beer barrels.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**Angelica’s favorite candies are those little root beer barrels.**

choices.content: Angelica’s favorite candies are those little root beer barrels.
Angelica likes candy; her favorites are those little root beer barrels.
Angelica likes candy, and her favorites are those little root beer barrels.

items.question_content:
**Combine the two sentences to make a logical _simple_ sentence.**

> We stood within the circle of Stonehenge. We watched the moon rise.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **simple** sentence:

* A **simple** sentence contains only one independent clause.

The simple sentence is

>**We stood within the circle of Stonehenge and watched the moon rise.**

choices.content: We stood within the circle of Stonehenge and watched the moon rise.
We stood within the circle of Stonehenge, and we watched the moon rise.
The moon rose, and we stood within the circle of Stonehenge.

khan_exercises.title: Complex and compound-complex sentences
khan_exercises.description: Complex sentences are simple sentences with dependent or subordinate clauses added to them. Compound-complex sentences are compound sentences with dependent or subordinate clauses added to them.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The complex sentence is

>**Although I have never made salami at home, I’m willing to give it a shot.**

The other sentence is *compound* because it contains two independent clauses connected by the coordinating conjunction "but".

options.explanation:
A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences.

Complex sentences will often use subordinating conjunctions like "because", "while", and "if".

choices.content: Although I have never made salami at home, I’m willing to give it a shot.
I’ve never made salami at home, but I’m convinced I have to try it.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound-complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The compound-complex sentence is

>**Noah peered into the bottle, but, although Diane kept trying to explain it to him, he couldn’t figure out what coconut aminos were.**

The other sentence is *complex* because it contains one independent clause and one dependent clause.

options.explanation:
A compound sentence contains two independent clauses, both of which can stand on their own as sentences. They often use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) or semicolons.

A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences. They often use subordinating conjunctions like "although", "because", and "if".

A compound-complex sentence has at least three clauses: two independent clauses and a subordinating clause.

choices.content: Noah peered into the bottle, but, although Diane kept trying to explain it to him, he couldn’t figure out what coconut aminos were.
Despite Diane’s repeated explanations, Noah couldn’t figure out what coconut aminos were.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The complex sentence is

>**As she was unable to swim, Lena didn’t want to go to the pool party.**

The other sentence is *compound-complex* because it contains two independent clauses connected by the coordinating conjunction "but".

options.explanation:
A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences.

Complex sentences will often use subordinating conjunctions like "because", "while", and "if".

choices.content: As she was unable to swim, Lena didn’t want to go to the pool party.
As she was unable to swim, Lena didn’t want to go to the pool party, but Jasmin convinced her to go.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound-complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The compound-complex sentence is

>**I’d like to visit Quebec, but because I don’t speak French, I’ll have to start learning.**

The other sentence is *compound* because it contains two independent clauses but no dependent clause.

options.explanation:
A compound sentence contains two independent clauses, both of which can stand on their own as sentences. They often use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) or semicolons.

A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences. They often use subordinating conjunctions like "although", "because", and "if".

A compound-complex sentence has at least three clauses: two independent clauses and a subordinating clause.

choices.content: I’d like to visit Quebec, but because I don’t speak French, I’ll have to start learning.
I’d like to visit Quebec, but I will have to start learning French for my trip there.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The complex sentence is

>**Since his pet parakeet flew away, Giovanni didn’t know how he was going to spend his Saturday.**

The other sentence is *compound-complex* because it contains two independent clauses connected by a semicolon.

options.explanation:
A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences.

Complex sentences will often use subordinating conjunctions like "because", "while", and "if".

choices.content: Since his pet parakeet flew away, Giovanni didn’t know how he was going to spend his Saturday.
Since his pet parakeet flew away, Giovanni was at a loss; he didn’t know how he was going to spend his Saturday.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound-complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The compound-complex sentence is

>**Although she was dismayed, Nicole said goodbye to her friends, and she set off to the campsite alone.**

The other sentence is *complex* because it contains one independent clause and one dependent clause.

options.explanation:
A compound sentence contains two independent clauses, both of which can stand on their own as sentences. They often use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) or semicolons.

A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences. They often use subordinating conjunctions like "although", "because", and "if".

A compound-complex sentence has at least three clauses: two independent clauses and a subordinating clause.

choices.content: Although she was dismayed, Nicole set off to the campsite without the friends she'd said goodbye to.
Although she was dismayed, Nicole said goodbye to her friends, and she set off to the campsite alone.

**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.

items.question_content:
**Which of these options is a _compound-complex_ sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
**Complex** sentences are sentences that contain *dependent clauses* (clauses that can't stand on their own):

* **Complex** sentences contain one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.
* **Compound-complex** sentences contain two or more independent clauses, and at least one dependent clause.

The compound-complex sentence is

>**In the early 20th century, a Louisiana congressman named Robert Broussard proposed a plan to fight an invasive plant species: he intended to introduce and farm hippos for meat in the Mississippi River, even though hippos are extremely dangerous animals.**

The other sentence is *compound* because it contains two independent clauses and no dependent clause.

options.explanation:
A compound sentence contains two independent clauses, both of which can stand on their own as sentences. They often use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) or semicolons.

A complex sentence has multiple clauses, but they can't all stand alone as sentences. They often use subordinating conjunctions like "although", "because", and "if".

A compound-complex sentence has at least three clauses: two independent clauses and a subordinating clause.

choices.content: In the early 20th century, a Louisiana congressman named Robert Broussard proposed a dangerous, innovative plan to fight an invasive plant species: he intended to introduce and farm hippos for meat in the Mississippi River.

In the early 20th century, a Louisiana congressman named Robert Broussard proposed a plan to fight an invasive plant species: he intended to introduce and farm hippos for meat in the Mississippi River, even though hippos are extremely dangerous animals.

items.question_content:
**Combine these parts to make a logical _complex_ sentence.**

1. When I go swimming
2. I have to keep my eyes closed underwater


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We're being asked to create a **complex** sentence. Remember:

>A complex sentence is made up of ONLY one independent clause and AT LEAST one dependent clause. 

When linking these clauses, we need to make sure the meaning of the sentence is clear!

The logical complex sentence is

>**When I go swimming, I have to keep my eyes closed underwater.**

The other sentence isn't structured in a way that makes sense.

choices.content: When I go swimming, I have to keep my eyes closed underwater.
I have to when I go swimming keep my eyes closed underwater.

The logical compound-complex sentence is

>**Although I drank a lot of coffee, I'm getting sleepy, and I don't think I'll make it through the movie.**

The other sentence isn't structured in a way that makes sense.

choices.content: Although I drank a lot of coffee, and I don't think I'll make it through the movie, I'm getting sleepy.
Although I drank a lot of coffee, I'm getting sleepy, and I don't think I'll make it through the movie.

**Combine these parts to make a logical _compound-complex_ sentence.**

hints:

The logical compound-complex sentence is

>**Although Lia gets scared very easily, she nevertheless loves to watch scary movies, and she will often stay up really late watching movie marathons.**

The other sentence isn't structured in a way that makes sense.
